narrative: As a white male applicant with a lower-middle family income background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile—including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 20.70 (well under the 36.70 average), Year 1 school decile of 3 (versus an average of 5.71), Year 3 decile of 2 (compared to 5.53), and part-time enrollment status—and calculated a 47% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to succeed on the bar from the outset. The three most influential factors in this decision were your LSAT score, Year 3 academic decile, and family income bracket. Your LSAT score had the strongest push toward the failure prediction, as it was significantly lower than what the model sees in students who typically pass. Similarly, your Year 3 decile ranking in the bottom portion of your school pulled the prediction further toward failure, signaling weaker performance relative to peers. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income actually worked in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting success, perhaps because students from similar backgrounds have shown resilience in passing the bar. Compared to typical applicants, who often have higher test scores, stronger decile rankings, and full-time status (92.7% of the dataset), your metrics stood out as riskier for bar exam outcomes. While these factors tipped the scales against you this time, they're not destiny—many students improve with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your later-year academics through additional coursework or tutoring, and exploring programs that value part-time students' real-world experience. Reapplying after addressing these areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor.
